sections.title: 1) What is Always-On? – “The brand voice should never be silent”

blocks.text: Always-on content is a content system that carries the brand's core identity, value proposition and trust elements independently of the campaign. The goal is not to “throw something away every day”; to continue the themes that define the brand throughout the year in repeatable formats. Always-on at the hotel; It is the trust-building layer such as experience, concept, UGC/reviews, destination and service standard. In B2B, frameworks, mini-lessons, case approach and expert opinion form the always-on backbone.
What is always-on content?

blocks.title: Answer
items: Always-on content; It is a regular and measurable content system that keeps the brand's story, value proposition and trust elements constantly visible outside of campaign periods.

blocks.text: Always-on content types (example):

items: Brand story + value proposition (who does what differently?)
UGC/comment/social proof (trust layer)
“Mini guide” contents (problem→solution)
Destination/experience narration (hotel)
Framework + checklist + insight (B2B)

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (Always-on intact?):
items: Do always-on content describe the “brand voice” in a single sentence?
Are there 2–3 repeatable formats each month? (carousel/reel/video)
Is UGC/social proof in order?
Are KPIs measured separately from the campaign?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Specify 4–6 content batteries for always-on.
Choose 2 format standards for each pillar (e.g. carousel + reel).
Plan a set of “trust” content each month (comments, case, proof).
Monitor Always-on KPIs in a separate dashboard.

sections.title: 2) What is Campaign Based Content? – “Burst periods”

blocks.text: Campaign-based content is a short-term period of concentrated content and distribution aimed at a specific goal (sales, launch, announcement, event). Early reservations at hotels, season openings, special day packages; In B2B, bursts such as product launch, webinar, new service package are typical. Campaign contents enable quick decisions; so the message, offer, proof and CTA should be more direct.
What is the difference between campaign-based content and always-on?

blocks.title: Answer
items: Always-on carries brand identity and trust in the long term; Campaign content focuses on sales/announcement targets in the short term. KPIs are also different: always-on (reach/engagement), campaign (click/lead/sales signal).

blocks.text: Campaign content components:

items: Offer clarity (what is it, for whom, on what terms?)
Evidence (comment, guarantee, case, before/after)
CTA clarity (single action)
Burst rhythm (short duration + high frequency)

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (Is the campaign ready?):
items: Is the proposal clear in 1 sentence?
Is there a single CTA? (DM/WhatsApp/form)
Are the 3 elements of evidence ready? (comment, image, condition transparency)
Do the burst duration and the number of contents match the capacity?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: For the campaign, write the "offer-evidence-CTA" trio on a single page.
Plan burst duration between 7–21 days (Assumption: depending on season/launch).
Set up the content set in 3 stages: teaser → proof → closing/CTA.
Report campaign KPIs separately.

sections.title: 3) Which one stands out in which situation? – “Decision matrix”

blocks.text: The mistake in many teams is this: when the campaign pressure increases, always-on is completely cut off; When the campaign ends, the account becomes silent. However, the best model is the one where always-on is the “base” and the campaign is added as a “burst” on top of it.
Should I share it constantly or in campaigns?

blocks.title: Answer
items: Make Always-on the base rhythm; Add short-term bursts on top of this during campaign periods. In this way, the brand voice is preserved and sales/announcement periods are strengthened.

blocks.text: Decision matrix (practical):

items: Brand new / low confidence → Always-on weight (evidence and value proposition)
Season/launch period → Campaign burst weight (sales/announcement)
Product/service complex → Always-on training + “clear package” in the campaign
Capacity limited → little but continuous always-on + small burst

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (Balance):
items: Does always-on “reset” during the campaign?
Does the account "go silent" after the campaign?
Are KPI targets separated by mode?
Is content production capacity exceeded?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Lock Always-on to “minimum weekly rhythm”.
Block campaign bursts in the calendar and pre-production.
Leave at least 1 always-on content per week, even during the campaign period (Assumption: based on capacity).
Add a 7-day “normalization” plan after the campaign.

sections.title: 4) Balancing Always-On and Campaign in the Annual Plan – “Block plan”

blocks.text: An annual plan allows you to manage campaigns in calendar blocks rather than “as you think of them.” Seasons are clear in hotels; In B2B, launches and events can often be planned in advance. The thing to do is: place the campaign blocks, fill the gaps in between with always-on, and pre-define the KPI set of each block.
How do I balance campaign and always-on in an annual social media plan?

blocks.title: Answer
items: First block the year's campaign/launch dates, then set goals+KPIs for each block. Fill empty periods with always-on pillars and fix content distribution according to production capacity.

blocks.text: KPI separation (net):

items: Always-on KPI: reach trend, engagement rate, save, profile action
Campaign KPI: click, lead/DM, form, sale/booking signal (Assumption: if measurement infrastructure exists)

blocks.title: Technical note (sheet):
items: Campaign intensity should not exceed technical/operational capacity and creative production power. Otherwise, quality will decrease, approval processes will be blocked and the campaign will turn into "too much content - little effect".

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (Annual plan):
items: Are campaign blocks marked with their dates?
Have targets/KPIs been written for each block?
Have always-on pillars been distributed in the spaces?
Is production capacity compatible with the weekly limit?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Separate campaign blocks by color on the 12-month calendar.
Add a “quote–proof–CTA” card to each block.
Rotate always-on content on a pillar basis.
Report by mode: always-on vs campaign.

sections.title: 5) Sample Calendars for Hotel and B2B – “Applicable distribution”

blocks.text: Seasonal blocks (early booking, summer season, holidays) are evident in hotel examples. In B2B, there are burst periods such as product/service launches, webinars and events. Recommendation in both scenarios: always-on's "base rhythm" should be constant, campaigns should be "planned burst".
Sample distribution (Assumption – initial model):

items: Hotel (seasonal): 60% always-on • 40% campaign (varies depending on the season)
B2B (launch focused): 70% always-on • 30% campaign (reversed during launch period)

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (Adapting the sample calendar):
items: Are your season/launch dates clear?
Can always-on pillars be produced “every month”?
Is there a campaign pre-production period?
Is the measurement and revision rhythm planned?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Start with a “simple distribution” in the first year and revise it in 90 days.
Power the best performing pillars always-on.
Increase the “quality of evidence” in campaign blocks, not the number of content.
Conduct a block-based retrospective at the end of the year.

faq.q: What is always-on content?
faq.a: It is a regular content system that carries the brand's story and trust elements in the non-campaign period. The aim is to maintain sustainable brand voice and community connection.

faq.q: What is the difference between campaign-based content and always-on?
faq.a: Always-on for long-term brand and trust; Campaign contents are designed for short-term sales/announcement targets. KPIs are also separate: always-on engagement is focused on campaign conversion signals.

faq.q: How do I balance campaign and always-on in an annual social media plan?
faq.a: First block the campaign/launch dates, write target+KPI in each block. Fill empty periods with always-on pillar rotation and lock in a weekly rhythm based on capacity.

faq.q: What should a sample annual plan for Hotel/B2B look like?
faq.a: Seasonal campaign blocks are evident in the hotel; always-on, on the other hand, lasts throughout the year as an experience/UGC/trust layer. In B2B, always-on thought leadership is the base, with short bursts added during launch periods.

faq.q: Should I share always-on during the campaign period?
faq.a: Yes. Always-on also balances the brand voice during the campaign and reduces the “billboard” perception. If capacity allows, leave at least 1 always-on slot per week.

faq.q: How should I separate KPIs?
faq.a: Engagement KPIs such as access/engagement/save for Always-on; Choose conversion KPIs such as clicks/leads/bookings for the campaign and keep reports separate.
